coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble pillar[5]arene modified amphiphilic graphene quantum dots with excellent penetration for intracellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Bing
Lu, Xingjian
Liao, Xiaokun
Zou, Hao
Tao, Junting
Ni, Chuyao
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: Amphiphilic graphene quantum dots (A-GQDs) reveal prodigious potential application in bioimaging, resulting from their hydrophilic and lipophilic properties, as well as their good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity. Herein, green fluorescent A-GQDs with single-layer graphene structure were prepared by one step of hydrothermal method. The A-GQDs with the average size of 3.5 nm displayed excitation-independent emission due to uniform size. A-GQDs emitted bright fluorescence on the surface of the cell membrane of MCF-7 cells when they were co-cultured with MCF-7 cells, which was similar to that of QSG-7701 cells. The water-soluble pillar[5]arene (WP5) was then incorporated on to the A-GQDs via π-π interaction and electrostatic interaction. There was different labeling imaging effect of MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells achieved by combining the merits of WP5 and A-GQDs compared with individual A-GQDs. The synthesized composites not only had a certain enhancement in fluorescence, but also constructed a penetrating channel through the cell membrane, so that the entire cell had a distinct imaging. Therefore, these composites might be a potential candidate for applications in visualization of cell membrane and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic graphene quantum dots
Pillar[n]arene
Penetration
Bioimaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of A-GQDs (insert: size distribution and high resolution TEM of A-GQDs). (b) AFM image of A-GQDs (inset: height of CDs). (c) TEM image of WP5/GQDs composites. (d) The hydrodynamic size of WP5/GQDs composites.
(a) Full XPS spectrum; (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) The FTIR spectra of WP5, A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs.

(a) Zeta potential. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra. Insets show the photographs of A-GQDs under irradiation of daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of A-GQDs. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of WP5/A-GQDs. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra of the WP5/A-GQDs with different weight ratios of WP5 and A-GQDs. (f) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs.

(a) The viability of MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of A-GQDs, WP5 and WP5/A-GQDs, respectively. (b) The viability of QSG-7701 cells treated with different concentrations of A-GQDs, WP5 and WP5/A-GQDs, respectively.
Fluorescent images of MCF-7 cells incubated with the same concentration of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs, respectively. The excitation wavelength was at 488 nm.
Fluorescent images of QSG-7701 cells incubated with the same concentration of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs, respectively. The excitation wavelength was at 488 nm.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble pillar[5]arene modified amphiphilic graphene quantum dots with excellent penetration for intracellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs.

Amphiphilic graphene quantum dots (A-GQDs) reveal prodigious potential application in bioimaging, resulting from their hydrophilic and lipophilic properties, as well as their good biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity. Herein, green fluorescent A-GQDs with single-layer graphene structure were prepared by one step of hydrothermal method. The A-GQDs with the average size of 3.5 nm displayed excitation-independent emission due to uniform size. A-GQDs emitted bright fluorescence on the surface of the cell membrane of MCF-7 cells when they were co-cultured with MCF-7 cells, which was similar to that of QSG-7701 cells. The water-soluble pillar[5]arene (WP5) was then incorporated on to the A-GQDs via π-π interaction and electrostatic interaction. There was different labeling imaging effect of MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells achieved by combining the merits of WP5 and A-GQDs compared with individual A-GQDs. The synthesized composites not only had a certain enhancement in fluorescence, but also constructed a penetrating channel through the cell membrane, so that the entire cell had a distinct imaging. Therefore, these composites might be a potential candidate for applications in visualization of cell membrane and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioimaging is of great significant mean to study complexity biosystems, which makes the process of diagnosis easier and visual [1–6]. Traditional fluorescent materials have been widely used in biological research, such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [7,8]. Despite their appealing capacities, there are still certain drawback, such as poor photostability against photobleaching for organic dyes and cytotoxicity for semiconductor quantum dots, respectively [9,10]. Two-dimensional reduced graphene oxideis were widely used in many fields [11,12]. Graphene quantum dots (GQD), with similar structure as zero-dimensional (0D) graphene with one or fewer layers and diameter less than 10 nm, have been considered as promising candidate applying to cell imaging in vitro owing to its low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, remarkable optical properties [13–17].

Most of unmodified fluorescent material which were commonly used in bioimaging possessed good water solubility. To improve the affinity to cell membrane and cell penetration ability, thus resulting in good cell imaging effect, many kinds of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) nanoparticles and conjugated polymer nanoparticles have been developed through physical cladding using amphiphilic polymer matrices (1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethan-olamine-N-methoxy(polyethylene glycol), DSPE-PEG5000 or poly(styrene-co-maleic anhydride), PSMA) as the encapsulation matrix [18,19]. However, intrinsic drawbacks such as complex preparation process and modification process are not conducive to their application. A water-soluble pillar[5]arene (WP5), as a new generation of supramolecular, has attracted great interest in its application in biological field, especially its unique cavity structure, which can capture neutral or cationic guests [20–23]. Owing to its intriguing property, WP5 combined with organic fluorescent material applying to biological analysis has been reported in previous research [21,24,25]. Ran et al. combined water-soluble pillar[6]arene with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots based on π-π interactions, constructing a sensitive electrochemical sensing platform with excellent supramolecular recognition capability for the detection of trinitrotoluene (TNT) [26]. But, there are relatively few reports mentioning that WP5 combined with carbon dots was used in biological field.

It is notable that many graphene quantum dots prepared by different raw materials are used in bioimaging, providing a lot of ideas. For instance, Qu et al. prepared water soluble multicolor graphene quantum dots through changing the reaction solvent with citric acid as carbon source and diethylene-triamine as nitrogen source [27]. Zhan et al. fabricated organic-soluble graphene quantum dots encapsulated by liposomes which had fluorescence imaging effect in the cytoplasm [28]. Nandi et al. constructed amphiphilic carbon dots, which had highly affinity to cell membrane without any modification and was of great value to investigate cell membrane [29]. In comparison with oil soluble or water soluble counterparts, amphiphilic GQDs have been the least studied in cell imaging due to their surface lack of abundant groups and weak fluorescence [30].

In this work, A-GQDs were synthesized by one step hydrothermal process under molecular fusion between 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and lauric acid. The A-GQDs displayed green fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 490 nm with maximum emission at 540 nm. The A-GQDs, which have been proved possessing low cytotoxicity, good photostability to pH and remarkable biocompatibility, were applied to MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells, exhibiting bright fluorescence imaging effect on the surface of cell membrane. For the first time, we then combined the A-GQDs with WP5 through π-π interaction and electrostatic interaction (Scheme 1 ). The hydrophobic alkyl chains of A-GQDs were crosslinked into a network structure by WP5, which reduced the affinity of the composites to the cell membrane. Water-soluble WP5 made the composites easier to penetrate into the two kinds of cells. What’s more, WP5 enhanced the fluorescence intensity of A-GQDs owing to the aggregation. Finally, the WP5/A-GQDs composites showed good fluorescence effect in totally MCF-7, QSG-7701 cells. Owing to the unique set of merits, the WP5/A-GQDs composites may have a great potential application in diagnostic and bioimaging.

WP5 was synthesized according to a previously published procedure(S1) [20]. Pyrene, NaOH and lauric acid were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was prepared by previously reported method [31]. 3-(4, 5-Dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were obtained from Hyclone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Penicillin-streptomycin solution were purchased from Gibco Co. Ltd. (USA). The aqueous solutions used throughout the experiment were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore).

The morphology and size of particles were conducted on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were obtained by atomic force microscopy XE-100E. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in the region from 4000 to 500 cm−1 on a Nicolet FT-IR 5700 spectrometer. The composition of A-GQDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements performed on Kratos Axis Ultra system. UV/Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were measured using Nano-ZS90. Fluorescence imaging were obtained by confocal laser scanning microscope (FV100-IX81) and cell viability was measured by microplate reader ELx800.

A-GQDs were prepared through hydrothermal method based on previous report with slight modifications [32]. Briefly, 1,3,6-Trinitropyrene (2 mg/ml) and lauric acid (0.025 M) were dispersed in NaOH solution (0.125 M, 20 ml) with ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the brown-black solution was dialyzed with cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da for 2 days against deionized water to remove unreacted small molecules. Subsequently, the dialysis was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. After being freeze-dried, brown powder of A-GQDs was obtained.

5 ml of 1 mg/ml A-GQDs aqueous solution was firstly prepared. The mixture was obtained after adding different weight ratio of A-GQDs and WP5 (from 1:1 to 1:4). The mixture was stirred overnight and the solution was filtered through a 0.22 um microporous membrane, and then further freeze-dried.

The cytotoxicity of the A-GQDs, WP5 and WP5/A-GQDs for cancer cell line (MCF-7) and normal cell line (QSG-7701) were measured by a standard MTT assay. MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells were cultured in DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in humidified 5% CO2 incubator. Firstly, 1 × 104 cells per well were seeded in 96-well plate (100ul per well) and cultured overnight. Then, the A-GQDs, WP5 or WP5/A-GQDs solutions with various different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250ug/ml, respectively) were added into each well and incubated for another 24 h. The wells without the adding A-GQDs, WP5 or WP5/A-GQDs were cultured as control. Thereafter, the culture medium was discarded, 20 uL of MTT (5 mg/ml) regent and 100ul fresh culture medium were added into each well, and further incubated for 4 h. Finally, discarding the supernatant and 150 uL DMSO was added into each well. The absorbance of each mixture was measured by microplate reader ELx800 at 490 nm.

The cellular imaging analysis of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs composites were examined using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The green channel was chose and the main parameters include: MultiAr(488 nm)laserunit100–240 V. In this experiment, MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells were seeded in cell culture dishes (35 mm dish a with 20 mm bottom well) and cultured for 24 h. Then, the medium was replaced with 2 ml fresh DMEM medium containing 100 μg/ml A-GQDs or WP5/A-GQDs composites. After incubation at 37 °C for 4 h, the supernatant was removed and the cells were washed with PBS three times. Then, 100 μl of 4% fixative solution was added into each dishes to fix cell morphology. Ten minutes later, the cells were further washed with PBS three times. Finally, the cells on cell culture were kept in PBS solution for confocal fluorescence imaging.

1,3,6-trinitropyrene, as an ideal active molecular precursor for preparing graphene quantum dots, has been proved possessing parent nucleus structure of graphene. Being introduced hydrophilic groups (–OH) from NaOH and hydrophobic alkyl chains from lauric acid, A-GQDs were synthesized through molecular fusion between 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and lauric acid by one step of hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene removed the nitro group (NO2 –) under the action of alkaline conditions and high temperature, forming a positively charged carbocation. Hydroxyl (OH–) in solution and lauric acid anion (C11H23COO−) combined with the positive carbon site to form a covalent bond. Finally, dehydration and carbonization occurred between the molecules to form A-GQDs. Besides, there was no precipitation or aggregation appearing to A-GQDs aqueous solution at room temperature in three months, indicating good water solubility and stability of A-GQDs. As uncovered in Fig. 1 a, TEM image revealed that the A-GQDs with an average size of 3.5 nm by measuring 100 particles was legible displayed with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) lattice plane of grapheme (inset of HRTEM image). The statistical particle size distribution of A-GQDs was between 2.8 and 4.2 nm. As shown in Fig. 1b, atomic force microscopy (AFM) image revealed the thickness of A-GQDs was ranged from 0.5 nm to 0.7 nm, indicating single layer of graphene structure. Pillar[5]arene could be combined with A-GQDs through π-π interaction between the phenyl group of WP5 and the single layer graphene core of A-GQDs [33]. To study the aggregation-induced change of particle size, the morphology and size of WP5/A-GQDs composites were further characterized by TEM and DLS (Fig. 1c-d). WP5/A-GQDs composites showed spherical shape with higher diameter of ~60 nm.

The composition of the as-prepared A-GQDs was further investigated by XPS. As indicated in Fig. 2 a, the full survey of XPS of A-GQDs showed two peaks at 283, 531 eV, confirming the presence of C and O with an atomic concentration of 83.05%, 14.94%, respectively. The full survey of XPS of WP5/A-GQDs composites showed three peaks at 283, 400 and 531 eV, which indicated that WP5/ A-GQDs consisted of C, N and O elements. The atomic concentrations of C, N and O are 89.1%, 9.45%, and 1.44%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peaks of the high resolution spectrum of C 1s which were centered at 283.5 eV, 283.9 eV, 285.3 eV and 287.6 eV corresponded to C-C=C, C-C, C-O, and O=C-O, respectively. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) presented two peaks at 530.6 and 532.5 eV, which were assigned to C=O and C-O, respectively. The structure of A-GQDs was further characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2d, the absorption bands of A-GQDs presented at ~3410 cm−1, 2928 cm−1, 2855 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 were attributable to –OH, –CH3, –CH2-, C=O/C=C stretching vibration. Based on the aforementioned analysis, hydrophilic –OH group and hydrophobic alkyl chain were proved to exist with graphitic structure. As for WP5/A-GQDs, several characteristic absorption bands could be observed, where absorption bands at 1484 cm−1, 2950 cm−1 and 3013 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of C-O-C and aromatic skeleton. Also, the peaks at 2928 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 of alkyl chain existed in the composites. These results demonstrated that WP5 was successfully composited with the A-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the zeta potential of the A-GQDs was measured to be −20.4 mV in deionized water due to the hydrophilic groups (–OH) while the value of WP5 was measured to be 2.92 mV as cationic quaternary ammonium salt. Because of electrostatic interaction between the hydrophilic groups (–OH) of A-GQDs and cationic quaternary ammonium salt of WP5, the as-prepared WP5/A-GQDs composites had the zeta potential of −0.201 mV.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission of the A-GQDs were measured, which were crucial issues for exploration of their potential applications in cell imaging. A very broad absorption was presented at the region <550 nm in Fig. 3b. The absorption peak at 457 nm was usually assigned to the n-π* transition of surface group or molecule connection at the carbon core edge [34]. It could be seen from the insert of Fig. 3b that A-GQDs aqueous solution emitted strong green fluorescence under UV lamp (365 nm) (on the right), and showed slight yellow color under daylight illumination (on the left). As shown in Fig. 3c, the maximum emission peak was centered at 540 nm under different excitation (400–490 nm), and the optimal excitation wavelength was 490 nm. While the emission peak intensity changed with the excitation wavelengths, the emission center of A-GQDs showed unchanged, implying excitation independent emission due to uniform size with a narrow distribution [16]. Also, The emission was related to surface states emission [35]. Fig. 3d presented that the WP5/A-GQDs composites possessed maximum emission wavelength (excited at 500 nm) at 546 nm. Compared with that of individual A-GQDs, the absorption and emission wavelengths of WP5/A-GQDs had a slight red shift in that the rigid symmetrical pillar architectures of WP5 increased the rigidity of composites particles (Fig. 3b-d). Fig. 3f showed that the fluorescence intensity of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs had almost no change when pH changed from 3 to 11, which also confirmed their good photostability. By measuring the change of fluorescence intensity, different weight ratios of A-GQDs and WP5 (1:1–1:4) were investigated. We found that the strongest fluorescence intensity was got when the ratio of A-GQDs and WP5 was 1:2, which was slightly enhanced compared with that of the individual A-GQDs (Fig. 3e). Because of the electrostatic interaction, the A-GQDs aggregated and the fluorescence increased [36].

Evaluation of the cell viability in the absence and presence of A-GQDs, WP5 and WP5/ A-GQDs composites is indispensable before applying them to cell imaging. Hence, the cytotoxicity was assessed by standard MTT assay with cancer cells (MCF-7) and normal cells (QSG-7701). As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the data collected with the different dose of A-GQDs, WP5 and WP5/ A-GQDs composites (0–250 μg/ml), demonstrated the negligible cytotoxicity towards MCF-7 and QSG-7701 cells. When the concentration was even up to 250 ug/ml, the viability of cells still maintained above 80%. Based on low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, the resulting conceived that A-GQDs and WP5/ A-GQDs composites could be used for cell imaging in vitro.

The fluorescence imaging effect of A-GQDs in MCF-7 cells in vitro was examined by confocal microscope under excitation wavelengths of 488 nm laser pulses. Fig. 5 exhibited that after incubation with MCF-7 cells for 4 h, most of the A-GQDs were distributed in cell membrane and a few A-GQDs were scattered in cytoplasmic area. Therefore, there was a bright fluorescent imaging effect on the surface of the cell membrane, and clear cell morphology was demonstrated. The structure of the prepared A-GQDs was distinct from the previously reported water-soluble carbon quantum dots [37]. For water solution carbon dots, the cell imaging effect would be different because the distribution of carbon dots in cells was uncertain. They can be distributed everywhere, such as cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, etc. [38–41]. However, the obtained amphiphilic quantum dots had lipophilic structure, exhibiting high affinity to the phospholipid bilayer of the cell membrane. They preferred to gather on the surface of the cell membrane.

In order to compare the imaging effects of A-GQDs and WP5/A-GQDs composites, the same amount of WP5/A-GQDs were introduced into MCF-7 cells. When A-GQDs compounded with WP5, the composite particles exhibited excellent biocompatibility and the entire cell showed a clearer image effect. This intriguing and different phenomenon was ascribed to the combination of the WP5 and amphiphilic graphene quantum dots which provided a pathway into the cell, allowing them to enter the cell interior easier. According to previous reported, the columnar structure of WP5 can be used as a channel for transmembrane transport [42]. Secondly, surface charge affects the internalization of particles in cells that positively charged particles can penetrate into cells more easily than negatively charged particles [43]. The zeta analysis showed that compared with A-GQDs in Fig. 3a, the zeta potential of WP5/A-GQDs composites were increased. The zeta potential of the A-GQDs was measured to be −20.4 mV. Because of electrostatic interaction between the hydrophilic groups (–OH) of A-GQDs and cationic quaternary ammonium salt of WP5, the as-prepared WP5/A-GQDs composites had the zeta potential of −0.201 mV. The above all hold WP5/A-GQDs composites could enter the cell more easily compared with A-GQDs. Moreover, the hydrophobic segments of A-GQDs were entangled with each other in the composite particles, which also reduced the affinity to the cell membrane and made them more easy to enter into the cell.

With the aim to explore the in vitro imaging application of A-GQDs in contrast of cancer cells (MCF-7), normal cells (QSG-7701) were selected as a cell imaging in vitro experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 , there was very bright fluorescence on the surface of the QSG-7701 cell membrane after incubation with individual A-GQDs. It also could be found that a few A-GQDs penetrated into the cytoplasm, which was similar to cancer cell imaging. Again, WP5/A-GQDs has also been tested for cell imaging ability against QSG-7701 cells which clearly delivered the same result to that of MCF-7 cells. The aforementioned analysis revealed that the lipophilic structure of graphene quantum dots and the columnar structure of WP5 was significant to cell imaging. Therefore, based on the above analysis, it is credible to state that the WP5/ A-GQDs composites have a great potential application in biomedicine and bioimaging.

In summary, we successfully developed A-GQDs from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and lauric acid by one step molecule fusion under hydrothermal method. The A-GQDs with single-layer graphene structure showed strong green fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, excitation-independence emission and good photostability to pH, which were in favor of excellent imaging in cells. The A-GQDs exhibited good imaging effect in MCF-7 cells and QSG-7701 cells. More A-GQDs concentrated in cell membrane and less distribute in cytoplasmic area. Due to its lipophilic property, the A-GQDs had a good affinity to cell membrane. The combination of WP5 and A-GQDs via π-π interaction and electrostatic interaction increased the penetration of composites into cell membranes, thereby enabling WP5/A-GQDs to evenly distribute in MCF-7, QSG-7701 cells. Besides, the composite particles still possessed excellent fluorescence properties. Finally, innovative combination of WP5 and A-GQDs will expand their application in visualization of cell membrane and bioimaging.

Bing Liu: Perform experiment and writing draft. Xingjian Lu: Revise and writing draft. Xiaokun Liao: Revise and writing draft. Hao Zou: Investigation. Junting Tao: Investigation. Chuyao Ni: Investigation. Jiaqi Pan: Give some recommendations. Chaorong Li: Give some recommendations. Yingying Zheng: Writing, review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.150739.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
